items.correct: Ideological incompatibility plus mutual suspicion after WWII — disputes over Eastern Europe, the atomic bomb, and broken wartime agreements.
items.misconception: Stalin started it by invading Eastern Europe after the war.
items.random_wrong: It began when the USSR shot down a US spy plane.
items.why: Students treat one side as solely responsible. Most historians now see the Cold War as a mutual breakdown — both sides acted on rational fears that the other read as aggression.

items.q: How was the Cuban Missile Crisis resolved?
items.correct: A secret deal: the USSR withdrew missiles from Cuba; the US publicly pledged not to invade Cuba and secretly agreed to remove Jupiter missiles from Turkey.
items.misconception: Kennedy stared Khrushchev down and the Soviets backed off unconditionally.
items.random_wrong: The crisis ended when Castro persuaded Khrushchev to withdraw.
items.why: The Turkey concession was kept secret for decades, fuelling the myth of unilateral US victory. The actual resolution was a negotiated compromise — important for understanding nuclear diplomacy.

items.q: What did the Truman Doctrine commit the US to in 1947?
items.correct: Supporting free peoples resisting communist takeover — initially Greece and Turkey — formalising the policy of containment articulated by George Kennan.
items.misconception: The Truman Doctrine declared war on the Soviet Union.
items.random_wrong: The Truman Doctrine ordered the dropping of the atomic bomb on Japan.
items.why: Containment was political and economic, not military invasion of the USSR. Students conflate Cold War rhetoric with hot war; Truman's doctrine was a framework for indirect rivalry.

items.q: In what year was the Berlin Wall built?
items.why: It's 1961. “1949” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Who led the Hungarian government during the 1956 Rising?
items.why: Correct answer: Imre Nagy. “Janos Kadar” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did the Korean War armistice end fighting?
items.why: The real figure is 1953. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
